0: Environmental impact of Danish pork at slaughterhouse gate - a life cycle assessment following biological and technological changes over a 10-year period
Teodora Dorca-Preda , Lisbeth Mogensen, Troels Kristensen, Marie Trydeman Knudsen Dept. of Agroecology, Aarhus University, Blichers Allé 20, P.O. BOX 50, DK-8830 Tjele, Denmark
1:
<list>The environmental impact of Danish pork was analyzed over a 10-year period 
Reductions in the environmental impact were found for all analyzed impact categories 
Biological and technological changes were identified in the pork chain 
Herd productivity increased while feed use per kg live weight gain was reduced 
Slaughterhouse utilization of live pigs increased from 79.4% in 2005 to 83.8% in 2016</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Pig production slaughterhouse LCA pork products for human consumption environmental impacts|Pork is one of the most consumed types of meat globally and it contributes considerably to environmental impact. The aim of this study was to investigate the development of the environmental impact of Danish pork between 2005 and 2016 in relation to biological and technological changes that have occurred at the primary production and slaughterhouse level at that time. A life cycle assessment approach from cradle to slaughterhouse gate was used, where Climate Change (CC), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Acidification Potential (AP), Abiotic Depletion (fossil fuels) (AD), Land Occupa- tion (LO), and Biodiversity Damage (BD) were assessed and expressed by three functional units (live weight, carcass weight and 'meat' for human consumption). A decrease was found in the environmental impact of Danish pork per kg 'meat' from 2005 to 2016 for all environmental categories, especially for CC (-31%), EP (-26%), and AD (-45%). This decrease was the result of several biological and technological changes that had occurred in the chain. At the primary production level, an increased herd productivity was documented: the number of pigs produced per SOW per year increased (total live weight gain from offspring per SOW per year was 3348 kg in 2016 compared to 2422 kg in 2005) and the feed use per kg live weight was reduced. These changes led to lower N excretion rates, which together with improved housing and manure handling strategies contributed to reducing the environmental impact of pork. At the feed production level, several technological changes decreased the impact. Firstly, reducing the N application rates for cereals and rapeseed led to reduced N emissions. Secondly, the use of new technologies for producing N fertilizers reduced the CC impact per kg N fertilizer. Finally, increasing the share of wind-based electricity in the Danish electricity mix caused decreases in the CC, EP, and AD impacts per kWh. At the slaughterhouse level, the increased utilization of the proportion of pig parts for human consumption reduced the total impact in the chain by 5%. Thus, it was identified as an important improvement option, with environmental and economic benefits. The improved utilization of by-products contributed to a reduction of more than 50% in the impact from the slaughterhouse. In terms of methodological choices, accounting for soil C changes, direct and indirect Land Use Change increased the CC impact in both years, but decreases were observed in 2016 compared to 2005. The need to include more by-products in pig feed rations was discussed in relation to LO and BD.
2: Pork is the most consumed meat globally (OECD, 2018). In the context of an increasing world population, pig farming including feed and manure management represents a major environmental challenge as it is responsible for greenhouse gas emission while also being a major player in soil and biodiversity degradation (Gerber et al., 2013; Stein- feld et al., 2006).

During the last few decades, there has been a constant focus in Europe on reducing the environmental pressure, and particularly on reducing the nitrogen problems, through regulation (EC, 2000; EC, 1991). To that end, a wide range of recommendations and requirements were proposed, which included bans on slurry spreading in the cold season, fertilizers budgets, crop-specific maximum limits on applications of plant-available N, statutory norms regarding the proportion of manure N assumed to be plant-available, establishment of catch crops, wetlands and buffer zones, mineral P feed taxes or protection zones around ammonia-sensitive habitats. Even though these were formulated to reduce nitrate water pollution and to protect the groundwater re- sources, many of the implemented recommendations are expected to have affected several other environmental aspects at the same time. The reason behind this is that pork production systems, similarly to all other livestock production systems, are very complex and therefore cause complex interactions in terms of both environmental impacts and pro- ductivity aspects. For example, interactions among feeding practices, manure storage, and land application were identified by Hristov et al. (2013) because of changes in the flow of N and C sources in the chain when implementing mitigation options. It is therefore important to understand the implications that different management strategies had on climate and the environment. This needs to be investigated because it could lead to the identification of valuable improvement prospects. These prospects would be valuable for two main reasons. Firstly, their identification would contribute to an analysis of recommended and feasible improvement options for pork production and other activities in the chain. Secondly, the analysis would provide insight into the changes made by farmers in production in response to the N regulations to ensure that their production is both feasible and economically viable.

In order to conduct an analysis in this sense, it is necessary to identify a representative pork production system. According to FAOSTAT (2018), the biggest pig exporters in Europe are Germany, Spain, Denmark, the Netherlands, and Belgium. Based on the production effi- ciency data (Wirsenius et al., 2020), Denmark can be considered representative for the analysis as it has the highest production efficiency among pork producers because of its high feed efficiency and low manure emissions. Additionally, Denmark was one of the first countries to implement measures to reduce the environmental load while also managing to ensure competitiveness in animal production (Dalgaard et al., 2004). In this context, the current status of Danish pig production (2016) can be assessed and compared to that from before the increased awareness regarding the environmental load of livestock. That aware- ness was brought by the FAO publication 'Livestock's long shadow' in 2006 (Steinfeld et al., 2006), which is also the reference point for the European Union's directives for livestock.

When investigating the environmental impact of livestock products, the whole-system perspective has to be considered (Hristov et al., 2013). One of the most widely accepted and commonly applied whole-system methods is life cycle assessment (LCA), which consists of a detailed environmental analysis of the production chain of a product. In a review study by de Vries and de Boer (2010) regarding LCA studies for livestock production, it was pointed out that interpretation of results can be enhanced if more environmental aspects (such as competition for land between animals and humans or land use changes) are included. These aspects are particularly important for pig production systems, as they make use of products that can be directly consumed by humans, such as cereals. Furthermore, Nguyen et al. (2012) showed that nature occu- pation (also known as 'land occupation" or 'land use') is the main

contributor to the environmental costs of pork production. Additionally, the correlations between the type of activities that are carried out in relation to land (for feed and food production) and the impact on biodiversity can be explored. It is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore biodiversity aspects as there are several initiatives that prioritize it (FAO, 2020; EC, 2011) and recommend its assessment. To the knowledge of the authors of this study, only a few studies focusing on pig products (Bonou et al., 2020; Bava et al., 2017; Dourmad et al., 2014; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2011; Noya et al., 2017) report the arable land area used in production, but they do not include biodiversity loss.

Given the above, it is important to estimate a wide range of envi- ronmental impacts to ensure that the results reflect a more accurate environmental profile, where some biological or technological changes might affect the environmental impact categories differently. This will furthermore reduce the risk of sub-optimization when identifying improvement options.

On this background, the aim of this study was to investigate the development of the environmental impact of Danish pork over time. The current status of pig production (2016) was compared to that from 2005, the reference point in the European Union's directives for livestock. Based on that, the most important biological and technological changes from pork production were identified. Furthermore, the most promising and feasible improvement options for pig production were determined and discussed.

8: The present study followed a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate attribu- tional LCA approach to investigate the development of the environ- mental impact of Danish pork production between 2005 and 2016 and to identify the most important biological and technological changes. The analysis of the changes between 2005 and 2016 looked for feasible management strategies that had been adopted, which occurred among other things because of N regulations and because of other changes introduced in production by farmers to maintain economic viability while following these regulations. The environmental impact in 2016 represents the current status of Danish pork production, while the impact in 2005 reflects the status of Danish pork production before the increased awareness of the environmental pressure of livestock brought by the publication by Steinfeld et al. (2006).
9: In the context of global population growth, pork production systems need to provide products that can feed the population while also focusing on reducing the environmental impact. For this reason, the functional unit for this study was 1 kg 'meat' at the slaughterhouse gate, defined as the total amount of pork products used for human con- sumption. It was considered that not only the on-farm operations but also the slaughterhouse level contribute to the overall results when expressed per kg 'meat'. For the interpretation of results, the impact was also calculated per kg carcass and per kg live weight by following the same reference flow.

The production stages from cradle to slaughterhouse gate that were included in the system boundaries were animal production (including animal rearing, manure management, and anaerobic digestion of manure and handling of waste), slaughtering process, (including in- transport of pigs, slaughterhouse operations, and handling of by- products in different production systems) and production of inputs such as feed and energy (Figure 1). The dotted line in Figure 1 is used to illustrate the substitution of production of inputs when using system expansion for the handling of multifunctional processes.
fur animals and pet feed production industry

Figure 1. System boundaries for Danish pork production included in the analysis (The main processes analyzed refer to animal production (including emission related to manure management and its anaerobic digestion), the slaughtering process (including the transport to slaughterhouse and utilization of by-products in other production industries and waste water treatment) and production of inputs (feed, fertilizers, energy))

2.1.2. Impact categories Given that the present study investigated the changes in production because of N regulations and those that occurred to ensure economic viability, the impact categories that were expected to be affected by the above factors were considered. These included: climate change (CC), eutrophication potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), land occu- pation (LO), biodiversity damage (BD), and abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (AD). The relevance of these impact categories in relation to livestock production is also exemplified by Steinfeld et al. (2006). In relation to climate change, the impact of soil carbon (C) changes and direct and indirect land use change (dLUC, iLUC) were also assessed and presented separately to gain more insight into their effect on the overall environmental impact. By including LO and dLUC, the present study also followed the recommendations by de Vries and de Boer (2010). The toxicity-based impacts (e.g., ecotoxicity) related to the use of chemicals such as pesticides, fertilizers, pharmaceuticals, etc. were not included in the present study because of data unavailability in 2005.

13: Pig production implies the production of piglets in sow-piglet units, followed by the production of weaners, and the production of slaughter pigs. Herd productivity data used in this study are presented in Table 1. The productivity data from 2016 were based on a farm dataset from 570 sow-piglet units, 541 weaners units, and 714 slaughter pigs units (Hel- verskov, 2017). A similar dataset (414 sow-piglets units, 414 weaner units, and 697 slaughter pig units) was used for the productivity data from 2005 (Vinther and Ostersen, 2009).

Based on the compiled data for primary production, input-output information was calculated for each of the production phases and pre- sented per 'year sow' (one SOW fed for 365 days) including its offspring (Table 2). The main inputs that were quantified were feed, bedding materials, and energy consumption. Production of gilts was also accounted for, as they replace the culled sows. Feed composition data

from Nguyen et al. (2011) were used to establish feed rations for 2016 and 2005, concomitantly making sure to fit the excretion levels of N and P reported in national norms (Poulsen, 2016; Poulsen, 2005). The en- ergy use in pig houses for the different animal categories was based on Nguyen et al (2011).

15: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

Emissions related to anaerobic digestion of manure were included in this analysis, by assuming monodigestion. The data regarding the technological process are based on Olesen et al. (2018) (Supplementary materials, Table S2). Emissions from biogas plants and saved energy were calculated according to Nguyen et al. (2010), while emissions for storage and application were based on Mikkelsen et al. (2016) and Hansen et al. (2008) respectively, as manure was returned, stored, and applied to fields at farm level (Supplementary materials, Table S1). The
CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was

CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
17: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). 18: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
20: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

estimates from Nielsen et al. (2016) for housing and Hansen et al. (2008) for storage and application. For NOx, the emission factors were based on Dämmgen and Hutchings (2008) and those for N2 were based on Vinther (2005) (Supplementary materials, Table S1).

Emissions related to anaerobic digestion of manure were included in this analysis, by assuming monodigestion. The data regarding the technological process are based on Olesen et al. (2018) (Supplementary materials, Table S2). Emissions from biogas plants and saved energy were calculated according to Nguyen et al. (2010), while emissions for storage and application were based on Mikkelsen et al. (2016) and Hansen et al. (2008) respectively, as manure was returned, stored, and applied to fields at farm level (Supplementary materials, Table S1). The
CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
22: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

Emissions related to anaerobic digestion of manure were included in this analysis, by assuming monodigestion. The data regarding the technological process are based on Olesen et al. (2018) (Supplementary materials, Table S2). Emissions from biogas plants and saved energy were calculated according to Nguyen et al. (2010), while emissions for storage and application were based on Mikkelsen et al. (2016) and Hansen et al. (2008) respectively, as manure was returned, stored, and applied to fields at farm level (Supplementary materials, Table S1). The
CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
24: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

Emissions related to anaerobic digestion of manure were included in this analysis, by assuming monodigestion. The data regarding the technological process are based on Olesen et al. (2018) (Supplementary materials, Table S2). Emissions from biogas plants and saved energy were calculated according to Nguyen et al. (2010), while emissions for storage and application were based on Mikkelsen et al. (2016) and Hansen et al. (2008) respectively, as manure was returned, stored, and applied to fields at farm level (Supplementary materials, Table S1). The
CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
26: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

(2008) respectively, as manure was returned, stored, and applied to fields at farm level (Supplementary materials, Table S1). The
CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated based on gross energy intake data determined by Nielsen et al. (2016) for 2005 and 2016 (Table 1) by following the IPCC (2006) approach.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
28: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). 29: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was

The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

2.2.4.3. Handling of multi-functional processes. Given that manure in Denmark is applied to crops as a substitute for fertilizers, manure was considered a valuable output and not a waste product in this study. For this reason, the implications related to the avoided production of N, P, and K fertilizers, N emissions at application, loading and spreading of manure, and soil C changes were calculated by using system expansion (Mogensen et al., 2014). The avoided production of N, P, and K fertil- izers was estimated based on substitution rates from Danish norms (75% for N and 100% for P and K) (Anonymous, 2016), by assuming that the substituted fertilizers are the most commonly used in crop production in Denmark (CAN, P2O5 and K20) (Mogensen et al., 2018). The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. According to Klitgaard Fries (2018) (personal communication), in 2016, fat was utilized in biodiesel production and the meat and bone meal were utilized in fertilizer production (Table 2). The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. (2007), while those related to avoided fertilizer production followed the same approach as for manure, with N, P, and K contents in meat and bone meal being documented from feed tables (Strudsholm et al., 1995). In 2005, the bone and meat materials were incinerated for energy production. The data used for accounting for this process are based on Dalgaard (2007).

Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). Fishmeal production is avoided when by-products are used for produc- ing feed for pets and fur animals (Mogensen et al., 2016). The dry-matter content in by-products was set at 19.5% (Padilikowa & Jezkova, 1984), and the environmental impact related to the production of fishmeal was
31: <header>2.2.2. Feed production </header>

In order to account for the differences in crop production between 2016 and 2005, the following production data were considered: yield (grains and straw), mineral fertilizers use and production technologies, energy use, and the area used for production. The starting point was the 2016 data for feed production from Mogensen et al. (2018). Given that the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs represent more than 90% of the feed rations (Table 2), the present analysis included year-specific production data for barley, wheat, and rapeseed, while for the other feedstuffs figures from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used for both 2005 and 2016. Fertilizer input and the yield of the Danish-produced crops (cereals and rapeseed) for 2016 and 2005 were set according to Danish Standards (Anonymous, 2016; Anonymous, 2005). In 2016, 60% of the Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) fertilizers used in the production of cereals were produced with new technologies, including catalytic cleaning of N2O (Elsgaard, 2015). In terms of land use typologies (Knudsen et al., 2017), the feedstuffs used in pork production are pro- duced in arable crop areas. The inventory data used for feed production in the present study are shown in Table 3.
2.2.3. Slaughterhouse

The main inputs at the slaughterhouse level are live animals and energy (Table 4). Data were obtained from the pork industry (Anony- mous, 2018; personal communication). For 2005, only the amount of energy used was known, and therefore the distribution into different types of energy sources was set to be the same as in 2016. Actual Danish electricity mix was used for 2005 and 2016 (EnergiNet, 2017). The transport figures for 2016 were based on data on the number of pigs and the location of farms and slaughterhouses taking into account that the

2.2.4.2. Estimation of emissions related to feed production. Emissions related to feed production were estimated according to the methodology used by Mogensen et al. (2014, 2018), which is presented in Table S3 from the supplementary materials. In the methodology described by Mogensen et al. (2018), all crops are assumed produced using only mineral fertilizers and the main emissions included are direct N2O from mineral fertilizers, indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching, NH3 from mineral fertilizers and crops, CO2 from lime, NO3 leaching, and PO4 leaching. The leaching of NO3 and PO4 leaching is estimated by using the mass balance method, where the surplus of N and P, respectively, is determined based on the N and P input and output at the field level for each crop. The NO3 leaching is then estimated as the difference between the N surplus and the sum of N emissions and N changes in the soil. The N changes in soil are calculated based on a C:N ratio of 10:1 and it is considered that 9.7% of the C input to soil from straw, above- and below-ground crop residues, and manure (if relevant) is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective (Petersen et al., 2013). The PO4 leaching is calculated to be 3% of the P surplus at field level. The environmental impact associated with feed is provided in the Supplementary materials, in Table S4. The emissions and environmental impacts related to pro- duction of inputs (such as fertilizers, energy use) were included based on databases (Supplementary materials, Table S5).

The energy use related to the application of fertilizers was based on Nguyen et al. (2010) (21 MJ t livestock manure and 0.4 MJ kg mineral fertilizer). The impact of applying manure instead of mineral fertilizers on soil C changes was accounted for as described by Mogensen et al. (2014).

At the primary pig production level, the distribution of environ- mental impact between the culled SOWS and the weaned piglets was carried out by using mass allocation. The allocation factor for piglets was therefore 84% in 2005 and 82% in 2016. With regard to the handling of dead animals, system expansion was used. The emissions related to avoided biodiesel production from animal fat were estimated based on Jensen et al. Given that most of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level are used in other production cycles, system expansion was per- formed to credit pig production systems for the avoided associated production (e.g. production of feed, medicines, and biogas) (Table 4). 34: The CML (2013) baseline impact assessment method was used to assess the environmental impact. That method was chosen as it follows the IPCC (2014) recommendations for characterization factors for CC. It must be noted that the characterization factor for biogenic CH4 for CC was changed to 25.25 (Ponsioen, 2014). For iLUC and dLUC, the methods by Audsley et al. (2009) and PAS2050 (BSI, 2008) were used, respectively. Soil C changes were estimated according to Mogensen et al. (2014).

Regarding the LO, the arable land area used per year for feed culti- vation in pork production was quantified. This was determined by dividing the area used for crop production (e.g., 1 ha) by the yield ob- tained in that area and reported as m² year The biodiversity damage potential was included based on the data from Mogensen et al. (2018), which follows the methodology by Knudsen et al. (2017). In that study, the fraction of species potentially disappeared (PDF) in an area and time frame is determined based on the relative loss in species richness. Occupation characterization factors are developed in relation to the number of vascular species present in woodland (natural forest in Europe) for different land use typologies (conventional pasture,

monocotyledons; organic pasture, monocotyledons; conventional pasture, mixed; organic pasture, mixed; conventional arable crop; organic arable crop; conventional hedgerows and organic hedgerows), based on data from six European countries from the 'Temperate Broadleaf and Mixed Forest' biome. The land use typology characteristic to the production of feedstuffs used in pork production is conventional arable crops. Mogensen et al. (2018) utilize a PDF of 0.68 for conven- tional arable crop fields, which reflects a 68% potential loss in species richness compared to the woodland.
