37: In order to account for uncertainty in the data used in the present study, sensitivity analyses were conducted. In relation to feed produc- tion, two sensitivity analysis scenarios were performed to account for uncertainty in the feed composition data. For this purpose, the ratio between cereals and protein-based products was increased and decreased by 5% (S1 and S2). Additionally, in the present study, average Danish productivity figures based on real farm data were used. For this reason, the obtained results do not capture the variation in input data between farms. In order to assess this aspect, data related to two major productivity parameters (mortality rates and the feed use per kg live weight gain for slaughter pigs) were collected from the same sources as the average production data for the 25% best (S3) and the 25% worst performing farms (S4) in 2016 and 2005 (Table 5). Based on these, the variation in the environmental impact within the years and between the years was analyzed.

The methodological choices are also aspects that can influence the results. Thus, IPCC (2006) methane conversion factors were used in S5 to capture the impact on the overall results of the use of methane con- version factors specific to Denmark. Furthermore, system expansion was performed in this study to handle the by-products in primary production and at the slaughterhouse level. Since the choice of allocation methods is known to influence the results significantly, two scenarios were set to examine these effects. The first one regarded the anaerobic digestion of manure in biogas plants. It can be argued that pig production systems are not credited with the subsequent energy production from the use of waste products in biogas plants and hence, a scenario (S6) was tested where the saved energy from biogas production was disregarded when accounting for the total environmental impact of the system. With re- gard to the handling of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse level, mass allocation was performed (S7) to illustrate the impact of system expansion on the overall results.

Secondly, the biological and technological changes that have taken place in Danish pork systems are identified and the environmental implications of the identified changes are discussed. 44: During the analyzed period, there was a decrease in the environ- mental impact of Danish pork per kg 'meat' in relation to all environ- mental impact categories, but especially for CC (by 31%), EP (26%), and AD (45%) (Figure 2). It must be noted that when soil C changes, iLUC, and dLUC were included, the impact of CC was higher (by 5, 28, and 29%, respectively, in 2016 and by 4, 22, and 24%, respectively, in 2005). Reductions by 8, 13, and 17% were observed in the contribution for these aspects in 2016 compared to 2005. The physiological pro- duction phase with the highest contribution (more than 60% of the impact for primary production) was the slaughter pig phase both in 2005 and 2016, but reductions on the pressure of each production phase were achieved. Compared to 2005, important reductions per kg 'meat' were
observed for the contribution from the sow-piglet units: -39% for CC, -45% for EP, -50% for AP, -32% for AD, and -36% for LO and BD (Figure 3).

The total CC impact per kg 'meat' was reduced by 31% from 4 kg CO2 eq., in 2005, to 2.7 kg CO2 eq., in 2016 (Table 6). Of the contributing factors, feed production and feed use had the highest decrease per kg 'meat' (10%) due to improvements in productivity and efficiency during crop production and feeding of the livestock. For manure management, an overall reduction by 18% was observed at the primary production level (Figure 4). At the slaughterhouse level, the impact on CC was decreased by 83% as a consequence of upgrading the transportation means, reducing the energy use per handled live pig, increasing the share of renewable energy in electricity production; utilizing by- products in other production processes (particularly rendering of by- products) and, not least, increasing the share of the pig live weight that is used for human consumption.

For soil C changes, iLUC, and dLUC reductions of 8%, 13%, and 17% were achieved per kg 'meat' (Figure 2). These were the result of increasing the productivity within the system of feed production and herd efficiency, as the composition of feed rations was very similar in the
Figure 4. Contributions to environmental impact of Danish pork of primary production in 2005 and 2016, % of total environmental impact of primary production
Figure 5. Contributions to environmental impact of Danish pork of slaughterhouse in 2005 and 2016, % of environmental impact at slaughterhouse level
two years. Increasing the share of pig LW parts used for human con- sumption contributed indirectly to the decrease as the output of 'meat' was increased with minimum change in inputs.

When analyzing the development between 2005 and 2016 for EP, reductions by 15 and 5 % were observed for the contribution from feed and manure management, respectively (Figure 4). Even though impor- tant reductions were achieved at the slaughterhouse level because of the developments related to energy use per tonne live pig, transportation and electricity production and through the utilization of by-products in fertilizer production, its share in the total impact for 'meat' was small
(Figure 5), except for the increased share of pig live weight used for human consumption.

Manure management and feed were also the main drivers of the reduction by 22% for AP between 2005 and 2016. The impact per kg 'meat' was decreased by 12% for manure management and by 9% for feed (Figure 4). The impact at the slaughterhouse was reduced by 73% in relation to AP due to the changes mentioned above, but with a very low effect per kg 'meat' (Figure 5).
AD was the impact category with the highest decrease per kg 'meat' (45 %) from 2005 to 2016. For primary production, the developments

regarding electricity production and the increased feed efficiency had positive impacts (Figure 4). Additionally, the change in practice regarding the rendering of dead animals and slaughterhouse by- products caused a drop of 8% for the total impact (Figure 5). Further- more, all these factors were influenced by the increase in the share of pig live weight used for human consumption in 2016.

With regard to LO and BD, there was a 14% decrease in the envi- ronmental impact per kg 'meat' (Table which was determined by the improvements in feed efficiency corroborated with improved herd productivity. This decrease was also the result of an increased share of pig live weight used for human consumption.

49: At the herd level, the number of litters and weaned piglets per SOW per year increased (by 1% and 28%, respectively) since 2005 (Table 1). At the same time, the mortality rates were reduced for piglets and slaughter pigs by 1% point. The live weight gain (LWG) of offspring increased per SOW per year from 2422 kg in 2005 to 3348 kg in 2016 and the feed use per kg LWG was reduced from 2.97 to 2.78 kg feed in the period from 2005 to 2016 (Table 2). Dead animals were incinerated for energy in 2005, while in 2016 they were used in biodiesel and fertilizer production (Table 2). This caused a decrease in the level of N and P excretions and subsequently reduced the level of N emissions per pro- duced animal. In this way, the environmental impact of Danish pork was reduced in relation to all impact categories (including soil C changes, iLUC, and dLUC) because of lower contributions from N emissions and feed (since feed production and use account for 56% of the total impact for CC, 78% for EP, 40% for AP, and it is the only driver for soil C changes, iLUC, dLUC, LO and BD in 2016) (Figure 4).
50: At the herd level, the number of litters and weaned piglets per SOW per year increased (by 1% and 28%, respectively) since 2005 (Table 1). At the same time, the mortality rates were reduced for piglets and slaughter pigs by 1% point. The live weight gain (LWG) of offspring increased per SOW per year from 2422 kg in 2005 to 3348 kg in 2016 and the feed use per kg LWG was reduced from 2.97 to 2.78 kg feed in the period from 2005 to 2016 (Table 2). Dead animals were incinerated for energy in 2005, while in 2016 they were used in biodiesel and fertilizer production (Table 2). This caused a decrease in the level of N and P excretions and subsequently reduced the level of N emissions per pro- duced animal. In this way, the environmental impact of Danish pork was reduced in relation to all impact categories (including soil C changes, iLUC, and dLUC) because of lower contributions from N emissions and feed (since feed production and use account for 56% of the total impact for CC, 78% for EP, 40% for AP, and it is the only driver for soil C changes, iLUC, dLUC, LO and BD in 2016) (Figure 4).
51: With regard to feed production, the N and P application rates per ha were reduced for the Danish cereal production from 2005 to 2016, while the yields were slightly increased (Table 3). This contributed to the reduced environmental impact of feed (Supplementary materials, Table S4) due to the reduced use of fertilizers and reduced N emissions. Given that feed is one of the main production hotspots for pork (Bava et al., 2017; McAuliffe et al., 2017; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2015; Reck- mann et al., 2013; Nguyen et al., 2011; Pelletier et al, 2010), it is important to emphasize that reducing the N application rates did not result in reductions in the level of yields (Table 3). That means that it was also economically viable for farmers.

Additionally, fertilizers obtained with new technologies were intro- duced in Danish crop production (60% of the market share in 2016). The use of these new technologies for producing N fertilizers halved the impact on CC per kg N fertilizer. Moreover, in the mix of (Danish) electricity generation, the consumption of wind-based electricity increased from 14% in 2005 to 40% in 2016 (Table 4), which caused a decrease by 38%, 33%, and 39% per kWh for CC, EP, and AD, respec- tively. No impact was observed on soil C changes, iLUC, dLUC, LO, and BD in relation to the above-mentioned changes.
52: With regard to feed production, the N and P application rates per ha were reduced for the Danish cereal production from 2005 to 2016, while the yields were slightly increased (Table 3). This contributed to the reduced environmental impact of feed (Supplementary materials, Table S4) due to the reduced use of fertilizers and reduced N emissions. Given that feed is one of the main production hotspots for pork (Bava et al., 2017; McAuliffe et al., 2017; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2015; Reck- mann et al., 2013; Nguyen et al., 2011; Pelletier et al, 2010), it is important to emphasize that reducing the N application rates did not result in reductions in the level of yields (Table 3). That means that it was also economically viable for farmers.

Additionally, fertilizers obtained with new technologies were intro- duced in Danish crop production (60% of the market share in 2016). The use of these new technologies for producing N fertilizers halved the impact on CC per kg N fertilizer. Moreover, in the mix of (Danish) electricity generation, the consumption of wind-based electricity increased from 14% in 2005 to 40% in 2016 (Table 4), which caused a decrease by 38%, 33%, and 39% per kWh for CC, EP, and AD, respec- tively. No impact was observed on soil C changes, iLUC, dLUC, LO, and BD in relation to the above-mentioned changes.
53: In the analyzed period, the average Danish housing conditions changed. For sows, the proportion of deep bedding systems used for pregnant SOWS were reduced from 30% in 2005 to 8% in 2016 and replaced by partly slatted floors. Similarly, for weaners and slaughter pigs, fully slatted floors were abolished and partly slatted floors were introduced. The share of manure delivered to biogas plants for anaerobic digestion increased from 7% in 2005 to 17% in 2016 (Table 1). These changes are particularly relevant for EP and AP, but they caused a decrease in the environmental impact also in relation to CC. An important aspect observed in relation to manure technologies was that

they influence the level of emissions (CH4, NH3, N20) both positively and negatively. For sow-piglet units, two different trends were observed in the development of manure emissions. Firstly, there was a significant decrease (58%) in the level of N2O emission per kg LWG due to lower N excretion rates and due to the replacement of deep bedding systems with partly slatted floors. Then, an increase by 53% in the level of CH4 emission was caused by the change in housing conditions. Those resul- ted in an overall decrease of 16% per kg LWG in the level of manure emissions for SOWS with piglets. For the fattening stage (weaners and slaughter pigs), the increased feed efficiency led to reductions in the level of these emissions of 2%.

Increasing the share of anaerobically digested manure had positive effects on CC and AD because of the substitution of energy production (Figure 4). For EP and AP, the level of reduction was lower than 1% in 2016 because NH3 emissions increase during the storage of digestate (Supplementary materials, Table S1). In this study, mono-digestion of manure was assumed and therefore, the implementation of these tech- nologies did not impact on dLUC, iLUC, LO, and BD. If co-digestion is used instead, the performance of biogas plants increases, but iLUC, dLUC, and LO are affected because of the production of co-digesters such as maize silage or beet tails (de Vries et al., 2012).

Contrary to expectations, implications for soil C changes were observed. In general, soil C sequestration figures are reported in pro- duction systems with grass-based feed rations (Knudsen et al., 2019). Given that pig production mostly makes use of concentrates, a release of C was estimated for the production of feed (Supplementary materials, Table S4) and therefore for Danish pork in 2005 and in 2016 (Table 6). However, according to the approach by Mogensen et al. (2014), when manure is applied on fields, 9.7% of its carbon content is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective. In this way, the C release from soils associated with feed production is decreased by the substitution of mineral fertilizers with manure. Therefore, if not taking into account the effect of substitution of mineral fertilizers with manure in feed pro- duction, the effect would be 48% and 41% higher in 2005 and 2016, respectively. Interestingly, in this analysis, the impact on soil C changes was reduced compared to 2005 because of increased feed efficiency in 2016. However, for the same reason, the excretion rates were lower and the effect of substitution of manure was reduced in 2016 (41%) compared to 2005 (48%).
54: In the analyzed period, the average Danish housing conditions changed. For sows, the proportion of deep bedding systems used for pregnant SOWS were reduced from 30% in 2005 to 8% in 2016 and replaced by partly slatted floors. Similarly, for weaners and slaughter pigs, fully slatted floors were abolished and partly slatted floors were introduced. The share of manure delivered to biogas plants for anaerobic digestion increased from 7% in 2005 to 17% in 2016 (Table 1). These changes are particularly relevant for EP and AP, but they caused a decrease in the environmental impact also in relation to CC. An important aspect observed in relation to manure technologies was that

they influence the level of emissions (CH4, NH3, N20) both positively and negatively. For sow-piglet units, two different trends were observed in the development of manure emissions. Firstly, there was a significant decrease (58%) in the level of N2O emission per kg LWG due to lower N excretion rates and due to the replacement of deep bedding systems with partly slatted floors. Then, an increase by 53% in the level of CH4 emission was caused by the change in housing conditions. Those resul- ted in an overall decrease of 16% per kg LWG in the level of manure emissions for SOWS with piglets. For the fattening stage (weaners and slaughter pigs), the increased feed efficiency led to reductions in the level of these emissions of 2%.

Increasing the share of anaerobically digested manure had positive effects on CC and AD because of the substitution of energy production (Figure 4). For EP and AP, the level of reduction was lower than 1% in 2016 because NH3 emissions increase during the storage of digestate (Supplementary materials, Table S1). In this study, mono-digestion of manure was assumed and therefore, the implementation of these tech- nologies did not impact on dLUC, iLUC, LO, and BD. If co-digestion is used instead, the performance of biogas plants increases, but iLUC, dLUC, and LO are affected because of the production of co-digesters such as maize silage or beet tails (de Vries et al., 2012).

Contrary to expectations, implications for soil C changes were observed. In general, soil C sequestration figures are reported in pro- duction systems with grass-based feed rations (Knudsen et al., 2019). Given that pig production mostly makes use of concentrates, a release of C was estimated for the production of feed (Supplementary materials, Table S4) and therefore for Danish pork in 2005 and in 2016 (Table 6). However, according to the approach by Mogensen et al. (2014), when manure is applied on fields, 9.7% of its carbon content is sequestrated in soils in a 100-year perspective. In this way, the C release from soils associated with feed production is decreased by the substitution of mineral fertilizers with manure. Therefore, if not taking into account the effect of substitution of mineral fertilizers with manure in feed pro- duction, the effect would be 48% and 41% higher in 2005 and 2016, respectively. Interestingly, in this analysis, the impact on soil C changes was reduced compared to 2005 because of increased feed efficiency in 2016. However, for the same reason, the excretion rates were lower and the effect of substitution of manure was reduced in 2016 (41%) compared to 2005 (48%).

57: At the slaughterhouse level, the share of pig live weight used for human consumption increased from 79.4% in 2005 to 83.8% in 2016 (Table 4) because of the expansion of the market to countries where diets also include pig products such as bones or some organs, which were not previously used for human consumption. Furthermore, the inputs used for handling animals at the slaughterhouse (energy, water) decreased, and the transportation means were upgraded. The handling of the by-products obtained at the slaughterhouse was changed to some extent in 2016, compared to 2005. The share of by-products used in the feed industry for pets and fur animals increased by 0.6% point and 1.5% point respectively while that for the by-products utilized in the phar- maceutical industry and biogas remained the same. As a consequence, the amount of rendered by-products was halved (Table 4).

The environmental impact at the slaughterhouse level dropped significantly for many impact categories (50% for CC and EP, 52% for AP, and 39% for AD) (Figure 5). However, most of the changes that took place within the system (utilization of by-products obtained at slaugh- terhouse level in other production industries, the use of renewable en- ergy sources, improved transportation means, and reduced energy use during handling of live animals) only had an important effect on AD. That is because, the slaughtering process only puts pressure on AD (25% of the total impact for Danish pig 'meat' in 2016), while it contributes less than 1% in all to the other impacts (Table 6). This is consistent with previously conducted pig studies (Reckmann al., 2013; Nguyen et al., 2011).

Nevertheless, the technological improvements related to increasing the share of pig live weight used for human consumption from 79.4% to 83.8% had considerable benefits for all the environmental impact cat- egories in 2016. In fact, when analyzing the development of the envi- ronmental impact between 2005 and 2016, the effect is 4-5% higher when expressed per kg 'meat' compared to kg LW or kg carcass (Table The reason for this is because there is an increase in the output with no change in inputs. This finding contradicts previous studies and shows that measures can be introduced at the slaughterhouse level to reduce the environmental impact of pork. Furthermore, this option is also economically advantageous as it means a higher utilization of the slaughtered pig.

60: In the present analysis, it was found that the Nitrates Directives (e.g., reduced application rates, transition to partly slatted floors, anaerobic digestion of manure) and the biological and technological changes (e.g., increased herd productivity, increased share of wind-based electricity, increased share of pig LW used for human consumption, environmen- tally upgraded transportation means) that occurred in Danish pig pro- duction contributed to a decrease in the environmental impact of Danish pork in all impact categories.

When looking at the whole range of changes identified in Danish pork production, interactions were observed in terms of their effect on reducing the environmental impact. For instance, in this study, the ef- ficiency of the implemented manure technologies was enhanced by lower N excretion rates, which were the result of increased feed and herd efficiency. That also means that the key for reducing the environmental burden for pig production could be a combination of measures of different types targeting several production aspects. That would be beneficial also because of the biological limitations coupled to pig pro- duction. Herd productivity can only be enhanced to the point where animal welfare is not affected. For this reason, it could be advantageous to combine the above-mentioned options with several others: reduced retention time in housing; slurry acidification; cooling of manure or use of urease, and nitrification inhibitors. These mitigation options were identified as effective for reducing greenhouse gas emissions by Hristov et al. (2013). Nevertheless, their combined effect has to be investigated from a whole-system perspective in relation to several environmental impacts, not only CC. As an example, it is important to investigate the use of nitrification inhibitors also in relation to toxicity. The nitrification inhibitors involve the action of chemical substances for reducing N emissions. These substances and other chemicals, such as the pesticides and fertilizers used in feed production and the pharmaceuticals administrated to the animals are known to contribute to toxicity-based impacts besides being beneficial in terms of emissions reduction or crop and animal protection. The whole range of consequences need to be assessed therefore to avoid pollution swapping. An example for this was found in the present analysis when investigating the impact on soil C changes. By increasing feed efficiency, the N excretion rates and the level of N emissions were reduced, but this also decreased the effect of manure application on soil C changes. Another aspect to take into ac- count is the economic feasibility. Combining several mitigation options could be costly for farmers and therefore that could impede their prac- tical implementation. Thus, it would be useful to provide insight also into the economic indicators when introducing (combinations of) environmental measures.

62: <header>3.3.2. The pressure on land and biodiversity </header>

When analyzing the whole range of environmental consequences, the environmental impacts with the highest decrease were CC, EP, AP, and AD. Nevertheless, one of the main concerns regarding livestock pro- duction systems, and also pig production systems, is their pressure on LO as they are important drivers in food-feed competition for land (Cassidy

et al., 2013). Even so, the smallest decrease in the environmental impact from 2005 to 2016 was that of LO and BD. Furthermore, when ac- counting for LUC indirectly (in relation to arable land use in general) the level of reduction was lower compared to accounting for LUC directly (by quantifying the release of CO2 associated with deforestation caused by the production of soybean meal). That means that there is a need for better management to reduce the pressure on land. To that end, de Vries and de Boer (2010) recommend the use of by-products from other agricultural industries. In this study, in 2016, only 2% of the total feed intake for slaughter pigs in Denmark was represented by by-products. As the human population grows it is expected that residues, by-, or co-products from food production systems will increase (Mottet et al., 2017). So, increasing the ratio of by- or co-products needs to be considered in pig feeding. While it is expected that the use of these products will reduce the impact on LO and BD, Mackenzie et al (2016) showed that including co-products in pig diets can result in environ- mental impact reductions for other impact categories. According to their calculations, the use of bakery meals is beneficial for CC, EP, AP, and AD, and using wheat shorts has a positive impact on CC and AD and does not influence the impact of EP and AP. Alternative feed ingredients such as those obtained from grass, microalgae, mussels, or insects (Lindberg et al., 2016) are other suggestions for reducing the pressure on land, but their environmental impact has not been documented extensively.

In the present study, increasing the share of pig parts used for human consumption from 79.4% in 2005 to 83.8% in 2016 caused a decrease by 5% for all environmental impacts. This can be considered a viable approach for reducing the pressure on land as more animal products are available for consumption without further increasing the production of pork. Unlike some recommendations for changing human dietary pref- erences (Smil, 2014), this option had already been put into practice, which means that there is already a demand for products such as pork organs. This is not only relevant for Denmark, but for livestock systems in general, as the above-mentioned products are mainly exported.

Irrespective of the measures that need to be introduced in livestock production to reduce the pressure on land, it has to be noted that the need for applying these measures was identified due to the inclusion in this study of a broad number of impact categories. Undeniably, the models used in the present analysis (and in LCAs in general) for assessing LO and BD are very simplified and they involve quantification of m² used in production for LO and assessment of species richness for BD. Nevertheless, it is very likely that the inclusion of indicators such as erosion resistance, biotic production, groundwater replenishment, habitat fragmentation, conservation values of species, species functional traits, or ecosystem services indicators will emphasize even more the need for better management in relation to LO and BD.

66: In the present study, it was found that combining increased feed and animal productivity with manure management technologies (anaerobic digestion of manure, partly slatted floors) and using inputs produced with new technologies (use of renewable energy sources, use of fertil- izers produced with catalytic cleaning, improved transportation means), while increasing the share of products used for human consumption contributed to reductions in the environmental impact of pork in rela- tion to CC, EP, AP, AD, LO, and BD. These aspects are important options towards achieving sustainability in livestock systems (Gerber et al., 2013), and therefore sustainability in food systems. However, emission intensities and production profiles vary widely at the global level (Gerber al., 2013), and thus specific management strategies need to be designed for different geographical regions. According to Mottet et al. (2017), productivity was greatly improved at the system level in Euro- pean production systems and additional marginal gains can thus be achieved through precision livestock farming or the development of feed additives. Furthermore, the optimized utilization of animal parts for human consumption can also be investigated. In regions characterized by high animal densities, but low animal productivity such as Latin

America, South Asia, or East Africa, it is expected that the imple- mentation of the options identified in the present study will lead to important benefits (Gerber et al., 2013). Relevant management strate- gies in these areas could include improving feed and herd productivity, better manure management, and energy-saving technologies. Since ru- minants dominate in these areas, it would be important to account for the soil C changes and BD related to grasslands and to analyze the benefits and the trade-offs related to other environmental impacts such as CC or EP.

With regard to the input data used in this study, it was observed that feed composition plays an important role in the environmental profile of 'meat' as the results for S1 showed benefits for all impact categories, while for S2 indicated the opposite (Tables 7 and 8). The production systems for protein-based feedstuffs (S2) result in a lower release of soil C to the atmosphere compared to cereals (S1) but are responsible for very high figures for dLUC because of soybean' production. Furthermore, when input data from the 25% best- and 25% worst-performing farms were considered (S3 and S4), only small variations in the environmental impact per kg 'meat' were noted both in 2005 and 2016. In 2005, there was a 1-2% variation for CC, soil C changes, and AD, a 3-4% variation for iLUC, dLUC, EP, LO, and BD and 4-5% variation for AP (Table 8). Similar variations were detected also for 2016 (1% for soil C changes, 2-3% for CC and AD, 3-4% for iLUC, dLUC, LO, and BD, and 5-6% for EP and AP) (Table 7), documenting a decrease in the environmental impact of Danish pork in 2016 compared to 2005.

In terms of methodological choices, the largest effect on the results came from the choice of allocation methods. The use of mass allocation instead of system expansion (S7) resulted in a decrease of 6-13% in all impact categories in 2016 and 15.7-17.6% in 2005. However, using this allocation method involves giving the same value to the products used for human consumption and the by- and co-products. The other

methodological choices were not as influential. The use of lower MCFs for slurry in S5 reduced the CC by 2% in 2016 and by 3.5% in 2005. S6 led to an increase of 1% for CC and 5% for AD in 2016 because of the exclusion of the displaced energy production from biogas. The change was almost unnoticeable in 2005 (0.3 % for CC and 1.3% for AD) because of a lower percentage of anaerobically digested manure compared to 2016.

S6 led to an increase of 1% for CC and 5% for AD in 2016 because of the exclusion of the displaced energy production from biogas. The change was almost unnoticeable in 2005 (0.3 % for CC and 1.3% for AD) because of a lower percentage of anaerobically digested manure compared to 2016.
75: This study provided a detailed analysis of the development from 2005 to 2016 of the environmental impact of Danish pork based on representative datasets from pig farms and specific slaughterhouse data. The environmental impact of Danish pork decreased in relation to all environmental impact categories, but especially for CC (-31%), EP (-26%), and AD (-45%). The decrease was the result of biological and technological changes identified in Danish pig production.

At the primary production level, increasing herd productivity, manure handling strategies, and feed production technologies were found to be the most important changes. At the herd level, increasing the number of live-born piglets per litter per year SOW from 13 to 16.3 and reducing the mortality rates for piglets and slaughter pigs by 1% point combined with improvements in feed efficiency by 13% and 7% for SOW- piglet units and weaners reduced the level of N excretion and therefore the level of emissions and the pressure of all environmental impacts. For manure management strategies, interactions were observed between CH4 and N2O emissions in relation to transiting to partly slatted floors in housing, but overall a decrease in the impact was found. The use of anaerobic digestion (mono-digestion) had positive effects on CC and AD and very little influence on EP and EP.

At the feed production level, the environmental impact was reduced due to lower N application rates for cereals and winter rape, use of new technologies for N fertilizers (especially in relation to CC), and increased

use of wind-based energy in the electricity mix. At the slaughterhouse level, a reduction of 5% for all impact cate- gories was achieved because of using a higher proportion of the slaughtered pig for human consumption, an aspect which is also economically advantageous. Several other improvement options were identified at this stage, but with low effect on the total impact of pork. For the further improvement of the environmental impact of pork, a combination of measures could be beneficial. The inclusion of a wide range of environmental aspects and economic indicators could provide a more comprehensive picture of the impact of pork and livestock system, in general.
